title: Support auto updates for Kubernetes workloads
body:
Add auto-update support to `podman kube play`.  Auto-update policies can    
be configured for:                                                          
 * the entire pod via the `io.containers.autoupdate` annotation             
 * a specific container via the `io.containers.autoupdate/$name` annotation 
                                                                            
To make use of rollbacks, the `io.containers.sdnotify` policy should be     
set to `container` such that the workload running _inside_ the container    
can send the READY message via the NOTIFY_SOCKET once ready.  For           
further details on auto updates and rollbacks, please refer to the          
specific article [1].                                                       
                                                                            
Since auto updates and rollbacks bases on Podman's systemd integration,     
the k8s YAML must be executed in the `podman-kube@` systemd template.       
For further details on how to run k8s YAML in systemd via Podman, please    
refer to the specific article [2].                                          
                                                                            
An examplary k8s YAML may look as follows:                                  
```YAML                                                                     
apiVersion: v1                                                              
kind: Pod                                                                   
metadata:                                                                   
  annotations:                                                              
      io.containers.autoupdate: "local"                                     
      io.containers.autoupdate/b: "registry"                                
  labels:                                                                   
    app: test                                                               
  name: test_pod                                                            
spec:                                                                       
  containers:                                                               
  - command:                                                                
    - top                                                                   
    image: alpine                                                           
    name: a                                                                 
  - command:                                                                
    - top                                                                   
    image: alpine                                                           
    name: b                                                                 
```                                                                         
                                                                            
[1] https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-auto-updates-rollbacks           
[2] https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/kubernetes-workloads-podman-systemd     
                                                                            
Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <vrothberg@redhat.com>                     

<!--

```release-note
Support auto updates for `podman kube play`.
```

comments.body: @edsantiago, I'd love to have your input on the tests.  They do not run fast for the known reasons.
Been looking. The refactoring is nice. Tests LGTM so far.
@edsantiago there was a flake in the new test on ARM.  I will keep an eye on it.  Please let me know if you see it flake another time.

You mean [this one?](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/6390523807137792/html/sys-podman-fedora-36-aarch64-root-host.log.html#t--00285)
```
         # #/vvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvv
         # #|     FAIL: container-specified auto-update policy gets applied
         # #| expected: '.*podman-kube@-tmp-podman_bats.GNDJSD-test.yaml.service,.* (test_pod-b),quay.io/libpod/testimage:20220615,false,registry.*' (using expr)
         # #|   actual: 'podman-kube@-tmp-podman_bats.GNDJSD-test.yaml.service,4107fa87fd9e (test_pod-b),quay.io/libpod/testimage:20220615,false,local'
         # #|         > 'podman-kube@-tmp-podman_bats.GNDJSD-test.yaml.service,e7cd7e8a0a90 (test_pod-a),quay.io/libpod/testimage:20220615,false,local'
         # #\^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```
It looks like the `registry` string is missing. That is not likely to be a one-off. Is there any way you can look into it before this merges?

> It looks like the registry string is missing. That is not likely to be a one-off. Is there any way you can look into it before this merges?

One container seems to have the wrong policy set.  Unfortunately, I do not manage to reproduce the issue at all.

The red cleanup warnings can be fixed with
```diff
index ea656e6f6..85560e2ef 100644
--- a/test/system/255-auto-update.bats
+++ b/test/system/255-auto-update.bats
@@ -504,7 +504,7 @@ EOF                                                                                                                                        
 
     # Clean up
     systemctl stop $service_name
-    run_podman rmi $(pause_image)
+    run_podman rmi $local_image $(pause_image)
     rm -f $UNIT_DIR/$unit_name
 }
 
```
And, I'm reliably seeing the `podman.io 404` error on my laptop (as root, not as rootless).

Something is nondeterministically pinging the registry. I think `--dry-run` is broken. This may or may not be the fault of this PR, but it has to be fixed before this PR merges. Again, sorry.

Confirmation: this change:
```diff
-    local_image=podman.io/image:test
+    local_image=localhost/localimage:$(random_string 10)
```
changes the flake error to:
```
Error: checking image updates for container [snip]: pinging container registry localhost: Get "https://localhost/v2/": dial tcp [::1]:443: connect: connection refused
```
(i.e., from `podman.io/404` to `localhost/ECONNREFUSED`)

@edsantiago PTanotherL. The issue was as follows. The `registry` policy instructs auto updates to look at the registry and check whether there's a new image.  Errors when looking up a new image were treated as fatal which I think is wrong - especially in an Edge use case.  The error is now non-fatal but logged.

I also massaged the test a bit and ironed out some farts in the code.
Looks like I need to re-add parts of the code I removed.  Will take care of that on Monday :^)
I think we're good to go
